title: Cincinnati · The Long Slide
subtitle: FBI UCR 1999–2019 + CPD incidents 2020–2026 · 50 neighborhoods · real locations

hook.line: Cincinnati crime, from the 2002 peak to 2019
hook.sub: FBI UCR · then the city's own count, month by month — up close

punchline.sub: −45% under the same FBI measure · CPD's own broader count begins in 2020

copy.seamExplain: The ruler changes here — not the city. Through 2019 the gray marks are the FBI's classic 'index' crimes. From 2020 the figures are CPD's own STARS incident records, which add the 'Part 2' offenses — a broader net, so the level steps up. Compare the shape within one color, never across the dashed line.
copy.chapter2Kicker: CHAPTER 2 · THE LAST 5 YEARS · CPD OPEN DATA
copy.chapter2Caption: Monthly incidents, 2021–2026. Every dot is a real reported incident location, block-level, sampled.
copy.transitionDesc: The chart counted per year. From here the map counts per month across 50 neighborhoods — every dot a real reported location, anonymized to the block.
copy.methodRecentSub: the last five years · month by month · real locations
copy.methodDotsSub: Every dot is a real reported incident's block — CPD publishes block-masked addresses. 94.9% of incidents map to one of 50 neighborhoods — the rest counted, disclosed, never invented.
copy.methodFootnote: FBI UCR (through 2019) and CPD's STARS incident records (2020+) are different measures — shown as two chapters, not one line.
copy.quizQuestion: Which Cincinnati neighborhood reports the fewest incidents?
copy.sourceLine: Data: Cincinnati Police Department via data.cincinnati-oh.gov · real block-level locations
copy.coverageText: 94.9% of records mapped to a neighborhood
copy.creditsSources: Sources: City of Cincinnati / Cincinnati Police Department reported-crime data (STARS, 2020–present; the datasets carry no explicit license — used with attribution) via data.cincinnati-oh.gov & FBI UCR (1999–2019) via the FBI Crime Data Explorer (ORI OHCIP0000; earlier FBI years are broken or non-contiguous in the archive and are omitted, never interpolated). CPD changed records systems in June 2024 — the two published datasets are deduplicated across the transition. CPD's Part 1 / Part 2 taxonomy is kept as published: robbery stays in Part 1 Violent, and 'Part 2 · All Other Offenses' is the source's own bucket, never presented as NIBRS. Neighborhoods: CAGIS official SNA polygons. Long-arc totals are citywide; the map counts the 94.9% mapped to a neighborhood. Basemap: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL).

historyNotes.text: 1999: 21,727 index crimes — the record starts here; earlier FBI submissions are broken or non-contiguous, so they're omitted, never patched.
2002: the peak — 28,803 index crimes; violent crime peaks the same year at 4,250.
2019: 15,718 — down 45% from 2002; the last FBI year.
2023: 26,238 — the high point of CPD's own broader count.

annotations.text: Every dot is a real reported incident location — block-masked addresses published by CPD open data.
July 2024 — the busiest month of the last five years: 2,365 mapped incidents.
Westwood — Cincinnati's busiest neighborhood for reported incidents over the last five years.
Downtown: mapped incidents up 66% since 2022 — the sharpest rise on the map.
Coverage note: after CPD's June 2024 records switch, 99.6% of 2025 incidents map to a neighborhood vs 93% in 2022 — later months look denser partly for that reason.
